2606.23443.title: What Does a Chemical Language Model Know About Molecules?
2606.23443.paper_summary: Chemical language models (cLMs) are widely assumed to learn surface-level syntactic patterns rather than learning meaningful molecular semantics. Here, we apply sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to MolFormer, an encoder-only cLM, to mechanistically examine how molecular representations are built across layers. We discover that early layers rely on position-tracking latents to parse molecular grammar, while later layers encode atom-in-substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Additionally, we show that non-canonical SMILES produce more disruptive representation shifts than invalid SMILES, driven by position-latent disruption propagating across layers. To support further exploration, we develop InterMol, an interactive visualizer for SAE activations on molecular strings and structures.
2606.23443.paper_summary_zh: 化學語言模型 (cLMs) 通常被認為是學習表面層的句法模式，而不是學習有意義的分子語義。<lb/>在這裡，我們將稀疏自編碼器 (SAEs) 應用於 MolFormer，一個僅包含編碼器的 cLM，以機械性地檢查分子表示如何在層之間構建。<lb/>我們發現早期層依賴於位置追蹤潛變量來解析分子語法，而後期層則編碼子結構中的原子和藥理相關特徵。<lb/>此外，我們顯示非典型 SMILES 產生的表示轉變比無效 SMILES 更具破壞性，這是由於位置潛變量的破壞在層之間傳播。<lb/>為了支持進一步的探索，我們開發了 InterMol，一個用於分子字符串和結構上 SAE 激活的互動可視化工具。
2606.23443.author: Christian Kenneth et.al.
2606.23443.authors: Christian Kenneth, Etowah Adams, Liam Bai, Gerard JP van Westen
